notable.notable_blurb: Preeminent abolitionist orator of his era

narrative_blocks.header: One speech made him abolition’s golden trumpet
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1837, the 26-year-old lawyer leapt to the stage at Faneuil Hall to denounce the men who excused the murder of abolitionist editor Elijah Lovejoy. The extemporaneous speech catapulted Phillips into the front rank of the antislavery movement, where he stayed for decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Abolition cost him his practice and his society
narrative_blocks.body: His marriage to abolitionist Ann Terry Greene and his embrace of the cause exiled Phillips from Boston’s elite and ended his law practice; his own family considered committing him as mad. He gave away over forty thousand dollars to those in need across his life.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept fighting long after emancipation
narrative_blocks.body: Unlike many allies, Phillips insisted that the vote and land were as vital as freedom, and he carried the fight into women’s suffrage, labor reform, temperance, and Native American rights. He ran, unsuccessfully, for governor of Massachusetts on a labor ticket in 1870.

narrative_blocks.header: He fought to seat women in London
narrative_blocks.body: At the 1840 World Anti-Slavery Convention in London, Phillips pressed, unsuccessfully, to seat the American women delegates whom the organizers had refused. Stanton and Mott later dated the birth of the women’s rights movement to that rebuff.

cross_connections.display_label: shares the 1881 Greenback-Labor Advocates print with him
his father, the first mayor of Boston
his sister, who married into the Reynolds medical family
a fellow abolitionist and suffrage leader in the same movement

burialCemetery.name: Granary Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: One of Boston's oldest cemeteries. Contains graves of Samuel Adams, John Hancock, Paul Revere, and victims of the Boston Massacre.
